questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which type of magnet consists of a coil of wire wrapped around an iron core and produces a magnetic field only when electric current flows through the coil?
questions.hint: This magnet requires electricity to work.
questions.explanation: An electromagnet generates a magnetic field when current passes through its coil. The field disappears when current stops. Option A (permanent) does not require current.

source_trace.content_anchor: consist of a coil of wire wrapped around the metal core made from iron. When the coil of wire conducts a current, a magnetic field is generated

explanations_tiered.definition: An electromagnet is a coil of wire, usually with an iron core, that produces a magnetic field only when electric current flows.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Electromagnets are temporary and controllable: the field strength varies with current, and polarity reverses with current direction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: An electromagnet is like a light bulb for magnetism — it works only when electricity is turned on.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The magnetic field B inside a solenoid is \(B = \mu_0 n I\), where n is turns per meter. An iron core increases B by factor of relative permeability \(\mu_r\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a toroidal electromagnet, \(B = \mu_0 \mu_r N I / (2\pi r)\). Hysteresis losses occur if the core is ferromagnetic and current is alternating.
reasoning_steps: Identify that a coil + core + current = electromagnet.
Note that magnetism exists only when current flows.
misconceptions: Thinking electromagnets are permanent is wrong; they require continuous current.
Believing the iron core is optional for strong fields is false; core greatly amplifies B.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Electromagnet: coil + iron core + current = magnetic field.

glossary_entry.definition: Magnet created by electric current in a coil, often with an iron core.

flashcard.front: What type of magnet uses electric current to produce a magnetic field?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which magnet type needs electricity to work?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: An electromagnet, which uses a current-carrying coil around an iron core.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Magnet that requires electric current to produce a magnetic field.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate by wrapping wire around a nail and connecting to a battery to pick up paperclips.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. An electromagnet requires current. Option A (permanent) does not.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is true about magnetic field lines inside a bar magnet?

options.A: They go from north pole to south pole
options.B: They go from south pole to north pole
options.C: They are absent inside the magnet
options.D: They are circular and do not connect to poles

questions.hint: Field lines form closed loops. Inside, they complete the path from the south back to the north.
questions.explanation: Inside a bar magnet, magnetic field lines travel from the south pole to the north pole, forming closed continuous loops. Option A describes outside the magnet.

source_trace.content_anchor: Inside the magnet, the direction of the magnetic field lines is from the south pole to the north pole

explanations_tiered.definition: Inside a bar magnet, magnetic field lines point from the south pole to the north pole.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary:
Field lines are continuous closed loops: outside N→S, inside S→N. This satisfies \(
abla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The magnetic field lines continue through the inside of the magnet, going from south to north to complete the loop.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Because magnetic field lines cannot start or end, they must travel inside the magnet from S to N. This is required by Gauss's law for magnetism.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The divergence-free condition \(\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = 0\) for a closed surface around a pole forces the flux to continue inside. The inside field is opposite direction to outside.
reasoning_steps: Recall field lines form closed loops.
Therefore inside must be S → N to close the loop.
misconceptions: Thinking there are no field lines inside is false; they exist but are not usually drawn in simple diagrams.
Believing inside direction is N→S is incorrect; that's outside.

glossary_entry.definition: Magnetic field lines inside a magnet travel from south pole to north pole.

flashcard.front: Which way do field lines point inside a bar magnet?
flashcard.back: From south pole to north pole.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Direction of B field inside a bar magnet?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Field direction inside a magnet from south to north, completing closed loops.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Inside: S → N. Outside: N → S.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw a complete loop with arrows: outside N→S, inside S→N.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Draw magnetic field lines around magnets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Inside: S→N. Option A describes outside direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.3 The earth's magnetic field and the compass
questions.question: Why is Earth's magnetic field crucial for life on Earth?

options.B: It deflects most of the solar wind, protecting the atmosphere

questions.hint: Think about what comes from the Sun that could harm living things.
questions.explanation: Earth's magnetic field deflects charged particles from the solar wind, preventing atmospheric erosion and reducing radiation at the surface. Option A (seasons) is caused by axial tilt, not magnetism.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 The earth's magnetic field and the compass
source_trace.content_anchor: Earth is surrounded by a magnetic field similar to the field around a bar magnet

explanations_tiered.definition: Earth's magnetic field forms the magnetosphere, which shields the planet from harmful solar wind particles.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The magnetosphere deflects most of the solar wind, a stream of charged particles from the Sun, protecting the atmosphere and life from radiation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Earth's magnetic field acts like an invisible shield that pushes away dangerous particles from the Sun.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Charged particles in the solar wind are deflected by the Lorentz force \(\mathbf{F} = q\mathbf{v} 	imes \mathbf{B}\) as they approach Earth, creating a bow shock and trapping particles in Van Allen belts.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The magnetopause stands off at ~10 Earth radii. Without this deflection, the solar wind would strip the atmosphere over geological timescales, as happened on Mars.
reasoning_steps: Recall that magnetic fields deflect moving charges.
misconceptions: Thinking the magnetic field blocks all radiation is false; some cosmic rays still penetrate.
Believing the field creates the ozone layer is incorrect; ozone is chemical, not magnetic.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Magnetic field protects from solar wind.

glossary_entry.definition: Region around Earth dominated by its magnetic field, protecting from solar wind.

flashcard.front: How does Earth's magnetic field protect life?
flashcard.back: It deflects charged particles from the solar wind.
flashcard.teacher_note: Show images of auroras as evidence of solar wind interaction with the field.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain importance of Earth's magnetic field for life
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Deflection of solar wind protects atmosphere. Option A (seasons) is due to axial tilt.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.4 Magnetic field of a current carrying conductor
questions.question: The magnetic field at a distance of 0.20 m from a long straight wire is measured to be \(5.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{T}\). What is the current in the wire? (\(\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{T·m/A}\))
questions.hint: Rearrange \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\) to solve for I.
questions.explanation: From \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\), solving for \(I = \frac{2\pi r B}{\mu_0} = \frac{2\pi (0.20)(5.0 \times 10^{-6})}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = 5.0 \, \text{A}\). Option A might come from forgetting to multiply by 2π.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Magnetic field of a current carrying conductor

explanations_tiered.definition: The current in a wire can be found from the magnetic field it produces using I = 2πrB/μ₀.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rearrange the Biot-Savart formula to solve for current when B, r, and μ₀ are known.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Rewrite the formula to get I alone, plug in numbers, cancel π, and compute.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This uses the same Biot-Savart integration as forward calculation but inverted. The factor π cancels, leaving I = (rB) / (2×10⁻⁷) after simplification.
worked_solution: Step 2: Multiply both sides by 2πr: B·2πr = μ₀I.
Step 4: Substitute r = 0.20 m, B = 5.0×10⁻⁶ T, μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷.
reasoning_steps: Substitute given values with correct units.
misconceptions: Forgetting to invert the formula correctly (using I = μ₀/(2πrB) instead) is common.
Not canceling π carefully can lead to arithmetic errors.

glossary_entry.definition: I = 2πrB/μ₀ for a long straight wire.

flashcard.front: Find I if B = 5.0×10⁻⁶ T at r = 0.20 m.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: B = 5.0×10⁻⁶ T at r = 0.20 m; find I.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce algebraic manipulation: B = μ₀I/(2πr) → I = 2πrB/μ₀.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate magnetic field of a current-carrying conductor
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 5.4 Magnetic field of a current carrying conductor
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. I = 2π(0.20)(5.0×10⁻⁶)/(4π×10⁻⁷) = 5.0 A. Option A (0.5 A) from omitting factor of 10.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.5 Magnetic force on a moving charge in a uniform magnetic field
questions.question: A particle with charge q enters a magnetic field B with velocity v at an angle θ between v and B. For which angle is the magnetic force exactly half of the maximum possible force?
questions.hint: F = qvB sinθ. Maximum when sinθ = 1. Half maximum when sinθ = 0.5.
questions.explanation: Maximum force is when sinθ = 1 (θ = 90°). Half of maximum occurs when sinθ = 0.5, which corresponds to θ = 30° (or 150°). Option C (45°) gives sin45° ≈ 0.707, not 0.5.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.5 Magnetic force on a moving charge in a uniform magnetic field
source_trace.content_anchor: F = qvB sinθ; when θ=0°, F=0

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic force F = qvB sinθ. Half maximum occurs when sinθ = 0.5 → θ = 30°.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The sine function ranges from 0 to 1. Solving sinθ = 0.5 gives θ = 30° (or 150°).
explanations_tiered.beginner: To get half the strongest push, the angle between the direction of motion and the field must be 30 degrees.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: F_max = qvB. Set qvB sinθ = ½ qvB → sinθ = 0.5 → θ = 30° or 150°. The acute angle is 30°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Because sinθ is symmetric about 90°, both 30° and 150° give the same magnitude. The direction of the force (perpendicular to both v and B) differs by sign.
misconceptions: Thinking 45° gives half force is wrong; sin45° ≈ 0.707.
Believing 60° gives half is incorrect; sin60° ≈ 0.866.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Half max force when sinθ = 0.5 → θ = 30°.

glossary_entry.definition: Angle θ = 30° at which magnetic force is half the maximum possible value.

flashcard.front: At what angle is magnetic force half of maximum?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What θ gives half max magnetic force?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: 30°, where sinθ = 0.5, producing half the maximum magnetic force.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Plot F/F_max vs θ to show the sine curve and the 30° point.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine magnitude of magnetic force on moving charge
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. sinθ = 0.5 → θ = 30°. Option C (45°) gives sin45° ≈ 0.707, not 0.5.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.6 Magnetic force on a current carrying conductor
questions.question: A wire of length 0.50 m carries a current of 2.0 A and is placed at 30° to a uniform magnetic field of 0.40 T. What is the magnitude of the magnetic force on the wire?
questions.explanation: \(F = ILB\sin\theta = (2.0)(0.50)(0.40)\sin30° = (2.0)(0.50)(0.40)(0.5) = 0.20\,\text{N}\). Option B might come from using sin60° ≈ 0.866 instead.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.6 Magnetic force on a current carrying conductor
source_trace.content_anchor: F = I L B sinθ; example: L=25cm, B=0.30T, I=6.0A, θ=90° → F=0.45N

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic force on a wire: F = ILB sinθ, where θ is angle between wire and field.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Only the perpendicular component of length relative to B contributes to force. At 30°, sin30° = 0.5 gives F = ILB/2.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply current, length, field, and the sine of the angle (0.5 for 30°) to get force.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Substitute: F = (2.0)(0.50)(0.40)(0.5) = 0.20 N. The sine factor reduces force from the maximum (which would be 0.40 N at 90°).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vector form: \(\mathbf{F} = I\mathbf{L} 	imes \mathbf{B}\). Magnitude = ILB sinθ. For L = 0.50 m, I = 2.0 A, B = 0.40 T, θ = 30° → F = 0.20 N.
worked_solution: Step 1: Formula: F = I L B sinθ.
Step 2: Identify values: I = 2.0 A, L = 0.50 m, B = 0.40 T, θ = 30°.

formula_analysis.name: Force on wire in B field
formula_analysis.derivation: From Lorentz force on conduction electrons.

misconceptions: Using sin90° = 1 instead of sin30° = 0.5 would give 0.40 N (Option C).
Forgetting to multiply by sinθ at all gives 0.40 N as well.

glossary_entry.definition: F = ILB sinθ; only perpendicular component contributes.

flashcard.glossary_definition: Force = ILB sinθ, less than maximum when θ < 90°.
flashcard.teacher_note: Show that at 30°, the effective length perpendicular to B is L sinθ = 0.25 m, then F = I (L⊥) B.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Calculate magnetic force on a current carrying conductor
flashcard.workbook_section: 5.6 Magnetic force on a current carrying conductor
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer A. F = (2.0)(0.50)(0.40)(0.5) = 0.20 N. Option C (0.40 N) would be for θ = 90°.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.7 Magnetic force between two parallel current carrying conductors
questions.question: Two parallel wires are separated by 0.10 m. Wire 1 carries 3.0 A and wire 2 carries 4.0 A in the same direction. What is the nature and approximate force per unit length between them? (\(\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{T·m/A}\))
questions.hint: Same direction → attraction. Use \(F/L = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}\).
questions.explanation: Same-direction currents attract. \(F/L = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(3.0)(4.0)}{2\pi (0.10)} = \frac{48\pi \times 10^{-7}}{0.2\pi} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N/m}\). Option B has correct magnitude but wrong force type.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.7 Magnetic force between two parallel current carrying conductors

explanations_tiered.definition: Force per unit length between parallel wires is F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd), attractive for same-direction currents.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The magnitude depends on product of currents and inverse of separation. Direction follows the right-hand rule.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Plug numbers into the formula: multiply the currents, then μ₀, divide by 2π times the distance.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This formula derives from B₁ = μ₀I₁/(2πd) and F₂ = I₂ L B₁, leading to F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd). The sign of force is positive (attractive) for same sign currents.
worked_solution: Step 1: Write F/L = μ₀ I₁ I₂ / (2π d).
Step 2: Substitute μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷, I₁=3.0, I₂=4.0, d=0.10.
reasoning_steps: Identify currents: both same direction → attractive.

formula_analysis.name: Force per unit length between parallel wires
formula_analysis.derivation: From B = μ₀I₁/(2πd) and F = I₂LB.

misconceptions: Thinking opposite currents attract is wrong; they repel.
Forgetting to divide by d correctly (using d²) is incorrect.

glossary_entry.definition: F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd); attractive if currents same, repulsive if opposite.

flashcard.front: F/L for wires with 3A and 4A, d=0.10 m, same direction?
flashcard.glossary_definition: Force per unit length between two parallel current-carrying wires.
flashcard.teacher_note: Define the ampere using this formula: 1 A is current that produces 2×10⁻⁷ N/m between two wires 1 m apart.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Describe the effects of magnetic force between two conductors
flashcard.workbook_section: 5.7 Magnetic force between two parallel conductors
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Attractive because currents same direction. F/L = (4π×10⁻⁷×3×4)/(2π×0.10) = 2.4×10⁻⁵ N/m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: How do hard disk drives (HDDs) use magnetism to store data?

options.A: By magnetizing tiny regions on a magnetic disk to represent 0s and 1s
options.B: By using electromagnets to physically punch holes in the disk
options.C: By using permanent magnets to hold the disk in place
options.D: By generating heat to change the disk's reflectivity

questions.hint: Digital storage uses binary — two states.
questions.explanation: HDDs write data by magnetizing small regions (domains) on a magnetic coating in one direction (1) or the opposite (0). Option B describes punch cards, not hard drives.

source_trace.content_anchor: Magnets alter the direction of magnetic material on a hard disk in segments that then represent computer data

explanations_tiered.definition: Hard disk drives store data by magnetizing small regions on a spinning magnetic platter in two opposite directions, representing binary 0 and 1.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A write head generates a local magnetic field that aligns magnetic domains. A read head detects the magnetization direction via magnetoresistance.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The hard drive writes data by making tiny magnets point one way for 0 and the opposite way for 1.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Each bit cell on the platter has a magnetic moment. The write head creates a field strong enough to switch the moment direction, encoding binary data.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Giant magnetoresistance (GMR) heads sense changes in electrical resistance as magnetic domains pass under the sensor, allowing very high data density.
reasoning_steps: Magnetism can encode two states: magnetization up or down.
Hard drives use this principle to store data.
misconceptions: Thinking hard drives use lasers (optical) is wrong; that's CDs/DVDs.
Believing data is stored as physical pits is incorrect for magnetic storage.

glossary_entry.definition: Recording data by magnetizing tiny regions on a medium.

flashcard.front: How do hard drives store data magnetically?
flashcard.back: By magnetizing small regions in opposite directions to represent 0 and 1.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does a hard drive store data?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: By magnetizing tiny regions to represent binary 0 and 1.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with SSDs (flash memory) which are not magnetic.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. HDDs use magnetization direction for binary data. Option B describes punch cards.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When two bar magnets are placed with opposite poles facing each other (attracting), how do their magnetic field lines behave in the region between the poles?

options.B: They converge and connect from one pole to the other
options.D: They cancel completely, leaving no field

questions.hint: Opposite poles attract. Field lines go from N of one to S of the other.
questions.explanation: For opposite poles (N to S), field lines emerge from the north pole of one magnet and enter the south pole of the other, forming continuous, converging paths. Option A describes repulsion (like poles).

source_trace.content_anchor: opposite poles of a magnet attract each other and bringing them together causes their magnetic field lines to converge (come together)

explanations_tiered.definition: When opposite poles face each other, magnetic field lines converge, connecting the north pole of one magnet to the south pole of the other.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The field lines form continuous paths from N to S across the gap, indicating attraction. Iron filings align along these connecting lines.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The field lines act like rubber bands pulling the magnets together, stretching directly from one pole to the opposite pole.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Superposition of the two dipole fields yields a net field that is strongest in the region between the poles, with field lines directed from N to S straight across the gap.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The magnetic scalar potential between opposite poles creates a nearly uniform B field in the gap if the poles are large compared to separation. Field lines converge smoothly from one face to the other.
reasoning_steps: Identify poles: N of one facing S of other.
Recall field lines go from N to S outside magnets.
Lines will connect directly across the gap.
misconceptions: Thinking opposite poles cause field lines to diverge is wrong; that's for like poles.
Believing the field disappears between opposite poles is false; it is strong and uniform.

glossary_entry.definition: Magnetic field lines that come together when opposite poles face each other, indicating attraction.

flashcard.front: What pattern do field lines show between opposite poles?
flashcard.back: They converge and connect from N to S across the gap.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Do field lines converge or diverge between opposite poles?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Converge (come together) because the poles attract.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Coming together of field lines between opposite poles, indicating attraction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show iron filings pattern for two magnets attracting — lines are dense and straight between poles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Draw magnetic field lines around magnets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Opposite poles → field lines converge (attraction). Option A describes repulsion (like poles).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.5 Magnetic force on a moving charge in a uniform magnetic field
questions.question: A student claims that a charged particle moving parallel to a uniform magnetic field will spiral outward due to the magnetic force. Why is this claim incorrect?

options.A: Magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity, causing circular motion, not spirals
options.B: For parallel motion, the magnetic force is zero, so no spiral occurs
options.C: Only neutral particles spiral in magnetic fields
options.D: Spirals occur only in electric fields, not magnetic fields

questions.explanation: When velocity is parallel to the magnetic field (θ = 0°), sinθ = 0, so magnetic force is zero. The particle continues in a straight line, not a spiral. Option A describes perpendicular motion (circular), not parallel.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.5 Magnetic force on a moving charge in a uniform magnetic field
source_trace.content_anchor: F = qvB sinθ; force is zero when v and B are parallel

explanations_tiered.definition: A charged particle moving parallel to a magnetic field experiences zero magnetic force because sin0° = 0.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The Lorentz force requires a perpendicular velocity component. Parallel motion yields no deflection.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you run exactly alongside a magnetic field line, the field doesn't push you sideways at all, so you don't spiral.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For parallel motion, θ = 0°, so F = qvB sin0° = 0. Without a perpendicular force, the particle continues in a straight line, not a spiral.
explanations_tiered.advanced: A helical path (spiral) occurs only when velocity has both parallel and perpendicular components. Pure parallel motion gives zero force, so no curvature.
misconceptions: Believing any motion in a B field causes a spiral is wrong; only motion with a perpendicular component does.
Thinking parallel motion causes circular motion is incorrect; that requires perpendicular velocity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Parallel to B → F = 0 → straight line.

glossary_entry.definition: No magnetic force acts on a charge moving parallel to a uniform B field.

flashcard.front: What happens to a charge moving parallel to a uniform B field?
flashcard.back: It continues in a straight line; magnetic force is zero.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does a charge moving parallel to B field spiral? Why?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No; F = qvB sin0° = 0, so straight line motion.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Falsely believing magnetic force acts on charges moving parallel to B; actually F = 0.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a diagram: v parallel to B → no force; v perpendicular → circular motion; v at an angle → helix.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe effects of magnetic fields on moving charges
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Parallel motion gives zero force (sin0° = 0), so no spiral. Option A describes perpendicular motion, not parallel.
